sentences.text: When they liquidated there, I don't know.
I was working in Madritsch.
When before, we went to Krakow-Plaszow, I didn't know where to go.
I was scared.
I said, where I can go?
This was when they liquidated.
But I was working before in Madritsch, you know.
But everybody, all the Jews has to be in ghetto.
Everybody has to be in ghetto.
But we went to work outside ghetto.
Now everybody has to be in ghetto.
Then they make clean-- clean from the Jewish people, you know?
I didn't know where to go.
And I said to my brother, I go with you.
He didn't-- this was the concentration camp, Krakow-Plaszow.
This was already for the people-- you know, they make barracks.
I remember the barrack where I used to live.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] before they liquidated the ghetto, it was different.
Wait-- I didn't tell you what we did.
They make an appel from all the girls, of all the womens, you know.
Before they liquidated this ghetto-- I didn't tell you this.
There was a big [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
All the Jewish they take on the train, the people who goes.
We didn't know where to go.
And to my girl friends, they said, Gienia, if you come to the front, go to the back again.
But here in the back you can go because the German police watch you.
And I went maybe two, three times I went back.
And they go.
There was 5,000 women.
They take the women, we didn't know where.
I went over there-- the Jewish police, the women, everybody, they got a room.
And I went over there.
This was before.
These 5,000 women they take to Stutthof-- in the ocean.
They got a big, big-- how you call the [NON-ENGLISH].
From a big-- everybody has to go, and they throw down in the ocean.
Yeah, we heard this.
They tell her.
They tell us.
No.
No Germans.
We hear from the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish police.
What they did. 5,000 they throw down in the ocean.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This was.
And we went back to the-- to the place where we used to live.
And then, when we go over there, they took us to Krakow-Plaszow.
Oh, they-- they take you.
They take you.
No, you have to walk, you know.
When we was in concentration camp, from concentration camp, from the barracks, we went, was down the factory-- Madritsch.
Over there, we went to work every single day.
When you go to work one day, we saw a big, big line [? from ?] of old people, young people.
And one guy was in a [NON-ENGLISH], like, you know, the very orthodox Jewish.
And it was a big, big hole.
And the hole burns, the fire-- a big fire.
Comes to him, to the orthodox Jew.
He saw he has to go, no-- no choice.
Before he went, he went, [NON-ENGLISH]-- and the hand to God, and fell down.
No, but he fell down.
You don't have to push.
She killed.
Or he was killed or not.
One time-- she killed.
The whole [NON-ENGLISH].
Burn, the fire, a big, big fire.
Yeah, this is a line.
This was a line.
This we saw a lot, a lot of times, like this.
But this time was a German woman in white gloves.
She killed the people.
No.
How did you know?
No-- we went by.
How can you go?
How can you look?
We was crying then.
It was terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, when we went to the concentration camp.
I used to-- we'd work-- everybody has to work.
Every single day.
This was in-- I worked in the morning.
This was-- you work a few hours.
It's not how many hours, how many hours they wanted, they keep you.
And was a [SIGHS] woman-- they didn't know nothing from sewing.
Nothing.
But they have to work.
And I was a supervisor, 45 machines.
I teach them the pants.
And it was over there, our guy, what he was by the men in the concentration camp.
This was like a Jewish guy.
I know him like now from Krakow, you know.
And the woman was with a little girl.
She was by the woman, his wife.
What she did-- she got a [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know how you call the [NON-ENGLISH] in German.
And she beat everybody.
Old, young, she beat you.
So terrible.
And the names she called.
And [SIGHS] there was one doctor at the concentration camp.
Was a big room for the sick people.
And if you was sick, you have to go.
Thank God, no.
Jewish, Jewish.
They hang him after the war.
In Krakow, they hanged him.
They wait for him-- oh, yeah.
He sent so many Jewish people in oven.
Terrible.
And was-- seven people came.
If you got jewelry or something, there, that guy, that Jewish guy, that-- that-- he was the master of all the Jewish.
You know, the-- the men.
He go to-- to Goeth, and tell them, they in there, he jewelry.
So they know who has the jewelry.
He took-- says everything to them.
Everything to them-- Goeth.
Was seven people.
One night we was in the factory.
Was one woman, she used to work [? for ?] us, too.
They take her out-- outside.
It was the-- the-- the-- the from the woman-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the big shot, what she call name the woman.
And she beat everybody-- her, her husband.
Seven people was.
They take them outside.
We didn't know what's gonna happen.
When they take them outside, we hear, boom, boom, boom.
They kill everybody.
Seven people.
Because he doesn't need you anymore.
He knows everything.
He doesn't need you, and they killed them.
And we have to go and look.
And they-- the one got a boy.
Was five years old.
He left someplace-- we didn't know where he is.
He is alive, but who knows where.
He is alive.
And the parents, and the uncle, and the aunt.
Everybody was killed.
Yeah.
Then we were separate.
I-- I went-- from Madritsch, those 5,000 people.
And that's a big factory. 5,000 people.
My husband, he was in the cutting room.
No, no, no.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, not the first.
Because I didn't see him.
No--
And I got married.
Yeah.
So-- so they know we know each other.
Berel, yeah.
So they make a list, what they going to-- Krakow-Plaszow has to go out.
Because the German are very close.
So they left.
So they take my husband, 35 people from 5,000.
They put me on the list.
I was very good.
You know, I was-- got the samples.
And they take me, my husband, about 30 of the-- the top in Madritsch.
And they put a list, and 35 people.
And they give the list to Schindler.
The factory.
No, the factory was German.
The-- the Madritsch was a German.
And the other guy was a German, too, but he was very, very good to us.
He'd bring packages.
He was a German.
Titsch-- Titsch.
His name was Titsch.
And Madritsch-- he was the second on Madritsch, you know.
He'd bring packages.
And my husband, the-- he got packages, too.
Then we saw a big, big fire.
And the sign was Auschwitz.
And just the women.
Yes.
Everybody-- all the women, there's 300 women.
All the woman goes-- we didn't know where we go.
No, no.
Not too far.
But we saw Auschwitz, and we says, finished.
No more alive-- we are finished.
Yeah, sure we knew.
So we came to Auschwitz-- was one side, and was two sides.
One was-- we didn't know-- we go to the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] know how you call-- take a bath.
Showers.
Yeah, we didn't know where we go.
But they know where they put you.
So we went in Auschwitz in the one.
This [NON-ENGLISH] was-- they don't give you gas.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other one, everybody was dead in the shower.
After the shower, you have to go--
Yeah, they took you everything what you got.
I was soaked.
I got nice clothes, nice boots like this.
They take everything and they give you one dress.
Not underwear, nothing.
One dress and shoes-- not shoes.
You know, the [NON-ENGLISH] shoes.
From [NON-ENGLISH].
From wood.
From wood.
And I was lucky I got small size.
But somebody got a big size they couldn't work.
No stocking, nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went to a barrack.
We got a special barrack.
Like you have to-- [NON-ENGLISH].
You have to, you know, when you see you-- you-- down and up, like a bed.
Up and down, up and down, you know, was the barrack.
This barrack, you couldn't go out, and nobody let you in.
No, you can't go in toilet-- no toilet, nothing.
No water.
What I did?
I don't know how I survived the three weeks.
There was one woman-- I know her from ghetto.
A young girl.
Maybe she got 18, 19 years old.
So she came and bringed me a bottle of water.
In the night-- everybody was sleeping.
Like 2:00, 12:00, 1 o'clock-- I was-- I says to myself, what's going to be is going to be.
I have to wash myself, I have to go in toilet.
I go-- it was a big, big like-- like two rooms here.
A latrine, what they call it in German.
It was closet-- the-- the closet.
Then was a shower.
I take a shower, and I take-- what I have to do I did.
And I went back.
If somebody catch you, no way.
They kill you.
And I was lucky.
I did almost every night.
And I was clean.
I was very clean.
And I find a comb-- the smallest, you know, with a comb you have no nothing in your hair.
The girl-- I-- we got a eight from that bread.
Eight from that bread-- this was-- like this.
You know, that piece.
I couldn't eat.
And we had the soup everyday like water.
But you have to eat, you know.
And I give her this bread.
She was pregnant.
Big girl.
By Jewish [INAUDIBLE], policemen are Jewish.
They send her in oven.
Maybe she was eight months.
But everything was like this.
They sent her in oven.
That's it.
This was three weeks.
Between the three weeks, they hear from Krakow-Plaszow, the women came, what they did.
They have to take a half of our liter blood from you.
And I didn't got blood.
I says, if they take me, I am finished.
I got the anemia.
And I says, I don't know what's going to be.
When I came, they have to take me.
I says, from doctor-- you know, doctor.
I don't feel so good today.
But he was sure I have the menstruation.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How up-- they didn't take me.
The girls, they passed away.
Not all the girls-- but the first girls, they couldn't take it.
They took a half of blood from you.
A liter.
A German-- a doctor.
No.
I was lucky I don't see him anymore.
And-- and here was a--a nurse by him.
